title: Hormuz Thaw: Chinese Tankers Exit as UAE Quit OPEC and Pipeline Bypass Accelerates
subhead: Three Chinese supertankers transited the Strait of Hormuz as Iran eased its blockade, but ADNOC warned full flows won't return until 2027. Meanwhile, the UAE dealt a shock to OPEC unity by quitting the cartel, and Russia-Belarus nuclear drills signaled escalating NATO tensions.

sections.heading: Hormuz Transit Thaws as Iran Coordinates Vessel Crossings
sections.body:
Three supertankers—two Chinese and one unidentified—exited the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, carrying a combined 6 million barrels of oil. The transit marks the most significant movement through the waterway since Iran's de facto blockade began in February, with Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps claiming to have coordinated the passage of 26 vessels in 24 hours.

Iran's Persian Gulf Strait Authority published a new map marking a controlled maritime zone stretching from Kuh-e Mubarak to south of Fujairah, requiring authorization for all transit. The moves suggest Tehran is selectively reopening the chokepoint—particularly for Chinese vessels—while maintaining leverage in peace negotiations.

Industry analysts caution it remains unclear whether the increased traffic represents a sustained thaw or a one-off. Daily transits have averaged around 10 vessels since the conflict began, down from more than 140 before the war. Two Chinese VLCCs—the Yuan Gui Yang (Cosco Shipping) and Ocean Lily (Sinochem)—are scheduled to arrive in China in early June.
sections.whyItMatters: The selective reopening appears calibrated to ease pressure on Chinese oil imports while maintaining Tehran's bargaining position with Washington. China receives roughly 45% of its oil through Hormuz.

sections.heading: ADNOC: Full Hormuz Flows May Take Until 2027
sections.body:
The head of UAE state oil firm ADNOC warned that even if the conflict ended immediately, full oil flows through Hormuz would not return until Q1-Q2 2027 due to infrastructure damage and logistical constraints. Sultan Al Jaber's assessment underscores the lasting impact of the three-month blockade on global energy markets.

Separately, the UAE's new crude pipeline bypassing Hormuz is now approximately 50% complete, according to ADNOC. The accelerated project aims to double the UAE's export capacity via Fujairah on the Gulf of Oman, providing a permanent alternative route outside Iranian control.

The pipeline construction reflects a broader regional push to reduce vulnerability to Hormuz disruptions, though the timeline means Gulf producers remain exposed to chokepoint risks for at least another year.
sections.whyItMatters: Even with a peace deal, the structural damage to oil logistics infrastructure means elevated prices and supply uncertainty will persist well into 2027, forcing importers to accelerate diversification strategies.

sections.heading: UAE Quits OPEC, Dealing Historic Blow to Cartel Unity
sections.body:
The United Arab Emirates announced its withdrawal from OPEC and OPEC+, dealing what analysts call a heaviest blow to the cartel's cohesion in decades. The UAE stated the decision reflects its "long-term strategic and economic vision" and the need to focus on "national interests."

Alongside Saudi Arabia, the UAE was one of only two OPEC members with meaningful spare production capacity—approximately 660,000 barrels per day, according to IEA estimates. ADNOC claims even higher capacity at 4.85 million barrels daily, close to its 5 million barrel target.

Oil prices rose more than 3% on the news, with Brent crude reaching $111.60 per barrel, before paring gains on the UAE announcement. Analysts at Rystad note that outside OPEC, the UAE would have both incentive and ability to increase production, raising questions about Saudi Arabia's role as market stabilizer.

The exit represents a victory for U.S. President Donald Trump, who has accused OPEC of "ripping off the rest of the world" and linked U.S. military support for Gulf states to oil prices. The UAE has also criticized fellow Arab states for insufficient protection during Iranian attacks.
sections.whyItMatters: The UAE departure structurally weakens OPEC's ability to manage global supply, potentially creating a more volatile oil market. With Iran and Iraq lacking spare capacity, Saudi Arabia becomes the sole swing producer—a position that may prove unsustainable.

sections.heading: Russia-Belarus Hold First Joint Nuclear Forces Drill
sections.body:
Presidents Vladimir Putin and Alexander Lukashenko personally oversaw Russia and Belarus's first joint nuclear forces exercise, with the two leaders participating via video conference. The drills involved Yars intercontinental ballistic missiles, Zircon hypersonic missiles, Sineva submarine-launched missiles, and Iskander-M tactical nuclear launches from Belarusian territory.

Russia's Defense Ministry said nuclear munitions were delivered to Belarusian field facilities as part of the exercise. Putin stressed nuclear weapons remain an "extreme and exceptional measure" while Lukashenko said the drills were defensive—though he noted countries possessing such capabilities "must know how to use them."

The timing is significant: NATO foreign ministers met in Sweden on Thursday to discuss sustained support for Ukraine, while Kyiv's Security Service announced enhanced security measures along the Belarus border amid concerns about a potential new Russian offensive from Belarusian territory.
sections.whyItMatters: The nuclear signaling is calibrated to deter NATO escalation over Ukraine while projecting strategic coordination between Minsk and Moscow. It comes amid Russian oil refinery disruptions from Ukrainian drone strikes and elevated energy prices from the Iran conflict.

sections.heading: Xi-Putin Summit: Energy Trade Booms but Pipeline Deal Stalls
sections.body:
Chinese leader Xi Jinping and Russian President Vladimir Putin oversaw the signing of more than 40 cooperation agreements in Beijing, declaring their partnership had reached "the highest level in history." Putin said Russia's oil exports to China grew 35% in Q1 2026, and bilateral trade reached approximately $228 billion in 2025.

However, the two sides reached only a "general understanding" on the long-stalled Power of Siberia 2 gas pipeline—a project that would deliver up to 50 billion cubic meters of Russian gas annually to China through Mongolia. No final agreement or timeline was announced.

The meeting came days after Trump's visit to Beijing, highlighting China's diplomatic balancing act. Xi called for a "complete cessation of hostilities" in the Middle East, warning that an early end to the conflict would help stabilize energy supplies and supply chains. A joint statement criticized "unilateral actions" that impede international shipping—an apparent reference to Hormuz.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia is positioning itself as a reliable energy supplier amid Middle East disruptions, but the failure to finalize Power of Siberia 2 reflects ongoing price disagreements and Beijing's caution about deepening energy dependence on Moscow while managing relations with Washington.

sections.heading: Russia's Oil Revenues Surge 39% from Iran War Price Rally
sections.body:
Russia's state oil and gas revenues are projected to rise 39% year-on-year in May to approximately 700 billion roubles, driven by elevated global oil prices from the Iran conflict. The windfall comes despite Ukrainian drone attacks that have forced central Russian refineries to halt or reduce production.

Major refineries in central Russia have stopped or cut fuel output following recent strikes, with Rosneft's Syzran facility in Samara Oblast—700km from Ukraine—among those hit. Ukrainian officials say their mid-range drone attacks are increasingly targeting Russian energy infrastructure to disrupt Moscow's war effort.

The paradox highlights Russia's dual challenge: higher export revenues from elevated prices, but growing domestic refining constraints from Ukrainian strikes and sanctions-related technology shortages.
sections.whyItMatters: Russia benefits from the Iran crisis through higher prices while facing increasing pressure on its own energy infrastructure. The 39% revenue surge helps fund the war in Ukraine, but domestic fuel supply disruptions present growing logistical challenges.

sections.heading: Australia's Arafura Secures Final Investment for Rare Earth Project
sections.body:
Australian rare-earth company Arafura announced it will begin construction in September on the country's first ore-to-oxide rare earth processing facility, backed by Gina Rinehart and the federal government. The project underscores Western efforts to build alternatives to Chinese-dominated rare earth supply chains.

The final investment decision marks a significant step in diversifying critical mineral processing away from China, which controls roughly 90% of global rare earth processing. The facility will produce neodymium and praseodymium—key elements for electric vehicle motors and wind turbines.

The timing is notable: Xi's summit with Putin featured criticism of Western "unilateralism" and "hegemonism," while China's dominance in rare earths has become a growing strategic concern for the U.S. and its allies seeking to accelerate energy transition while reducing Chinese dependency.
sections.whyItMatters: Western governments are investing heavily in critical mineral supply chains as energy transition accelerates, but the infrastructure gap with China remains vast. The Arafura project is one of several attempts to bridge that gap over the next decade.

bottomLine: Chinese supertankers exiting Hormuz suggest Tehran is selectively easing its blockade, but ADNOC's warning that full flows won't return until 2027 and the UAE's shock departure from OPEC signal permanent changes to global oil architecture. The Iran conflict has accelerated both infrastructure diversification (UAE's bypass pipeline, Western rare earth projects) and cartel fragmentation. Meanwhile, Russia is capitalizing on elevated prices while conducting nuclear signaling against NATO, and Xi-Putin talks produced energy trade growth but no breakthrough on the Power of Siberia 2 pipeline. The energy geopolitics of 2026 are being rewritten in real time—with implications that will outlast any ceasefire.
sources: SCMP, Reuters, Al Jazeera, AP News, BBC, Daily Sabah, Nikkei Asia, Economic Times, Anadolu Agency
